title: Real-time Krylov Diagonalisation for Open Quantum Systems
abstract: In this chapter we present how real-time quantum subspace methods can be modified to simulate open quantum systems in Lindblad form. We apply these methods to a two-photon driven superconducting Kerr resonator and show how they allow to estimate the Liouvillian gap in the Kerr Cat qubit regime.   This is based on work done between November 2025 and January 2026 and on the talk given in early February 2026 at the Kwekfest 2026 conference, to celebrate L.C. Kwek's lifetime achievements.
summary: This paper presents a method to use real-time Krylov subspace diagonalization to simulate open quantum systems. It successfully applies this technique to estimate the Liouvillian gap in Kerr cat qubits, demonstrating that dissipative evolution can be used to filter out unwanted modes.
main_problem: Extending real-time Quantum Subspace Diagonalisation (QSD) methods from closed Hamiltonian systems to open quantum systems described by Lindblad master equations, specifically addressing numerical instabilities caused by non-normality.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that QSD can faithfully reconstruct the Liouvillian gap and Wigner representations in the Kerr Cat qubit regime, showing that dissipative dynamics can act as a spectral filter to suppress fast-decaying modes.
method: Real-time Krylov Subspace Diagonalisation (QSD) using a Generalized Eigenvalue Problem (GEVP) approach applied to superkets, implemented via Trotterization and Quantum Imaginary Time Evolution (QITE).
model_or_system: A two-photon driven superconducting Kerr resonator in the Kerr Cat qubit regime, modeled by a Lindblad master equation featuring Kerr nonlinearity, detuning, and single-photon loss.
key_observables: Liouvillian gap, photon number, field amplitude expectation value, and Wigner representations of eigenstates.
important_parameters: Kerr nonlinearity (K), two-photon pump strength (g), detuning (delta), photon loss rate (kappa), and Krylov dimension (D).
main_assumptions: The method requires doubling the quantum register size to encode density operators, and the implementation assumes the ability to perform deep circuits and frequent trace estimations.
figures_summary: Figure 5 illustrates the quantum circuit architecture for simulating dissipative dynamics using Trotterized unitary circuits combined with QITE.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the extension of QSD to Lindblad dynamics, discusses the mathematical challenges of non-normality, applies the method to the Kerr Cat qubit model, and evaluates the accuracy and circuit implementation of the algorithm.
why_interesting: It provides a NISQ-friendly, unbiased alternative to QPE for studying dissipative dynamics and the emergence of protected subspaces in driven-dissipative systems, which is crucial for developing error-protected cat qubits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Intuition from the complex circle: there is a tradeoff between frequency/energy resolution by increasing τ and satisfying the Nyquist sampling criterion, to avoid time-evolved superkets to wind around the complex circle and cause artifacts like aliasing. (b) Intuition from complex spirals: instead of clustering around the complex circle, the time-evolved (right) superkets spiral inwards. Fast decaying modes rapidly sink into the centre of the unit disk. τ is not constrained by the Nyquist’s criterion. The slow modes tend to survive for longer and they are captured in the Krylov representation (c) Pictorial representation of the effect of small perturbations on the Liouvillian...

FIG. 2. The Kerr Cat Regime coincides with the onset of the Liouvillian gap closing. This is because a slow mode, corresponding to the coherences and population imbalances, becomes long-lived. The gap is reduced as ∝exp −α2, and corresponds to the phase-flip rate. At very high drive, the symmetry is restored and the gap reopens. Inset: Wigner representation of the slow mode |ρSM).

FIG. 3. (a) The Liouvillian spectra and associated reconstructions in different regimes (color code: orange ▽, green +, red ×). (b) Different gap reconstructions for increasing evolution time τ. We see that increasing τ results in better estimates, and that bad estimates are off by a larger amount when the Liouvillian commutator increases. Each experiment is repeated three times. (c) Non-normality measured by the commutator of the Liouvillian and its adjoint.

FIG. 4. Long times allow for a faithful reconstruction of the gap across several orders of magnitude. In this simulation we have access to the (900 × 20) Krylov matrix which can be used to reconstruct the Wigner representation of the Liouvillian eigenstates. Insets: Real and reconstructed Liouvillian eigenstates |ρSS) and |ρ1)(left: damped regime, center: in the biased- noise Cat regime the slow mode features two lobes, right: strong drive, squeezed regime).

FIG. 5. To simulate dissipative dynamics with a quantum computer, one can Trotterise reversible unitary circuits with QITE for the Lindbladian dissipator D[l]:(adapted from ref. [45]). Caveats: (i) the number of qubits doubles, (ii) long-time evolution is needed while keeping error low: deep circuits are needed, (iii) non-unitary evolution is implemented with QITE, so the superkets remain normalised, and (iv) initial states must be valid density operators of trace 1.
